preFix.title: Hot composting horse manure for garden use
preFix.subtitle: Turning stable muck into finished compost in 8–12 weeks
preFix.excerpt: Fresh horse manure mixed with straw bedding is the classic hot-composting feedstock, high nitrogen, high carbon, already in a manageable mix. A well-turned stable-muck heap reaches 65 to 70°C within 3 days, kills weed seeds and pathogens, and produces finished dark compost in 8 to 12 weeks. For anyone with access to horse manure, stables often give it away, this is one of the fastest routes to large volumes of finished compost.
preFix.sourceNotes: ADAS Nutrient Management Guide (RB209) for horse manure nitrogen content. RHS guidance on composting stable manure 2024. Jeff Lowenfels, Teaming with Microbes (2010).

content.text: Fresh horse manure from straw-bedded stables has a
of roughly 25 to 30:1, almost exactly the ideal for

. The straw provides the carbon; the manure provides the nitrogen. If you know someone with horses, a load of stable muck in spring or autumn is one of the fastest routes to finished compost available to a home gardener.

Some stables use wood shavings rather than straw. Wood shavings have a C:N of 400 to 500:1, far too high for balanced hot composting. If you receive manure on wood shavings, add a nitrogen activator (fresh grass clippings, urine, a nitrogen fertiliser) at roughly one part activator to five parts muck by volume, or compost it cold for 12 months.

A hot compost heap must be at least 1 m × 1 m × 1 m to retain enough heat to reach and maintain thermophilic temperatures. Smaller heaps lose heat to the surface too quickly. A typical car-boot-sized load of horse manure (roughly 0.5 m3) should be combined with other materials to reach the minimum volume before attempting hot composting; otherwise cold-compost it.
Day 0: build the heap. Insert the compost thermometer to the centre. The temperature should begin rising within 12 to 24 hours.
Day 3 to 4: when the centre temperature reaches 65 to 70°C, turn the heap. Move the cool outer material to the centre. The pile should re-heat after each turn.

Turn every 3 to 5 days for the first 3 to 4 weeks, maintaining the temperature above 55°C. After 5 turns with the centre reaching 55°C each time, the material has met the US standard for thermophilic pathogen reduction (15 days above 55°C with multiple turns).
Leave to cure for 4 to 6 weeks after the active phase ends. The pile stops re-heating after turning; curing finishes the breakdown of complex compounds and matures the compost.

Finished horse manure compost smells earthy and sweet, not ammoniacal or sulphurous. The original straw is no longer identifiable as individual straws. The material is dark brown to black, crumbly, and holds its shape when squeezed but breaks apart easily. A germination test (press a few radish or cress seeds into a small pot of the material and compare germination to seeds in potting compost) confirms it will not inhibit planting.
